evals.name: reviewer-role
evals.expected_output: A markdown review response following the review-response.md template: verdict (APPROVE/CONCERNS/BLOCK), reason, structured findings with file:line refs (must catch SQL injection on line 14 and truthy-array bug), 'What I checked' and 'What I did NOT check' sections.

assertions.text: Response includes a 'Verdict' section with one of APPROVE, CONCERNS, or BLOCK
Response identifies the SQL injection where payload.sub is interpolated into the query string
Response identifies the truthy-array bug (db.query returns an array; 'if (!user)' is always falsy)
Response uses file:line reference format for at least one finding (e.g., 'src/auth/middleware.ts:14')
Response includes a 'What I checked' or equivalent section listing inspected scope
Response includes a 'What I did NOT check' or equivalent honest-scope-limits section
Response addresses the injection-risk focus question (one of the three the implementer raised)

evals.name: implementer-handoff
evals.prompt:
Ok I just finished adding rate limiting middleware to our Express API — it's in src/middleware/ratelimit.ts, about 80 lines, uses a Redis-backed sliding window approach. Before I merge this I want Codex to take a look because the failure modes around Redis are tricky.

Specifically I want them to focus on: (1) what happens if the Redis connection drops mid-request, (2) whether we handle clock-skew between the API node and the Redis node correctly, and (3) whether the bucket key derivation is correct for authenticated users vs anonymous IPs.

Can you set up a proper handoff so I can ship the packet to Codex via CLI? Walk me through the commands. I have the diff staged in git against main.
evals.expected_output: A walk-through showing: (1) staging a packet with SUMMARY and QUESTIONS env vars, the user's specific focus areas embedded in QUESTIONS, against the 'main' base ref; (2) the request-review.sh codex /tmp/packet.md invocation; (3) maybe a description of the verdict/response handling. Should not fabricate the file content the user didn't provide.

assertions.text: Response references the stage-packet.sh script (or describes its function clearly)
Response references the request-review.sh script targeting 'codex' (or describes invoking codex CLI directly)
Response uses 'main' as the base ref for the diff, as the user specified
Response embeds the three focus questions (Redis drop, clock skew, bucket key) into the packet/QUESTIONS, not just summarized away
Response does NOT fabricate the contents of src/middleware/ratelimit.ts (the user didn't share the code)
Response mentions the verdict tiers (APPROVE/CONCERNS/BLOCK) or the response-handling flow

evals.name: bridge-onboarding
evals.prompt:
Hey — me and a collaborator are using two AIs on our codebase. Sometimes I drive Claude, sometimes she drives Codex. We keep stepping on each other's toes because neither AI has visibility into what the other did. I want to set up a proper review handoff between them — like, when Codex finishes implementing something, Claude should review it before we merge. And vice versa.

What's the cleanest way to do this? Walk me through the actual setup — installation, the first review, what a normal day looks like.
evals.expected_output: A clear walk-through covering: (1) the AI Collab Bridge concept, (2) installation (git clone into ~/.claude/skills/ or similar), (3) the two roles (reviewer / implementer) and that the protocol is bidirectional, (4) the verdicts APPROVE/CONCERNS/BLOCK, (5) a typical daily flow showing stage-packet.sh and request-review.sh being used.

assertions.text: Response names the AI Collab Bridge by name (or its purpose) as the proposed solution
Response describes installation via git clone (to ~/.claude/skills/ or a similar path)
Response explains BOTH roles (reviewer AND implementer) and that the protocol is bidirectional
Response mentions all three verdicts: APPROVE, CONCERNS, BLOCK
Response shows stage-packet.sh used in the daily-flow example
Response shows request-review.sh used in the daily-flow example
